ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Roasted Pork Noodle; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for Singaporean roasted pork noodle; A0FJP (noodle-based dish with meat) is the closest defensible category-level anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for this dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Roasted Pork Noodle in Singapore is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of egg noodles topped with sliced roasted pork belly, dressed with dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and lard, often accompanied by a small side of soup.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Pork Noodle — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Pork Noodle (烧肉面), cr_id G00010705. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly roasted data (USDA SR Legacy #10006) proportionally adjusted to the fat content reported by HPB. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Egg Noodles and Mixed Noodle Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., 'International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021', American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB-reported per-100g data for this dish records carbohydrate as 0.0 g/100g, which reflects the meat-dominant (roasted pork) component of the dish rather than the complete dish with noodles. Because the carbohydrate content of the analysed portion is 0 g/100g, the glycemic load per serving based on this nutritional record is 0.0. However, the dish as consumed includes egg noodles, which carry a meaningful glycemic load. For reference, egg noodles have a GI of approximately 52 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021). The GI value of 52 is assigned as an analogous estimate reflecting the noodle component; the GL per serving is reported as 0.0 consistent with the HPB-reported 0 g carbohydrate per 100g for this record.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content at 1050 mg per 100 g, driven by dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoned noodle sauce, with negligible potassium offset (47 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request minimal sauce and a reduced-sodium preparation, and do not exceed a half-portion serving to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The HPB-analysed portion records 0 g carbohydrate per 100g (reflecting the meat component), but the complete dish with egg noodles carries a significant refined-carbohydrate load; high sodium may also impair fluid balance and indirectly affect glycaemic management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion or substitute with whole-grain or lower-GI noodles, monitor total carbohydrate intake from the noodle component, and limit sauce volume to reduce sodium-driven fluid retention.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) elevates blood pressure risk; roasted pork belly contributes saturated fat (~3.8 g/100g) and cholesterol (89 mg/100g); lard used in noodle tossing adds further saturated fat load.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request preparation without lard, choose lean pork cuts without skin, and limit sauce volume to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Roasted pork is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory cytokines; high-temperature roasting generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); sodium at 1050 mg/100g worsens joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute roasted pork with chicken or fish, remove pork skin to reduce AGE and saturated fat load, and request minimal sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Pork Noodle (烧肉面; Siu Yuk Mee) is a popular Singaporean Chinese noodle dish featuring slices of siu yuk — Cantonese-style crispy roasted pork belly with crackling skin — served over springy egg noodles tossed in a savoury dressing of dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and lard. The dish is typically accompanied by a small bowl of clear pork broth and may include blanched vegetables such as choy sum or bean sprouts. The roasted pork is characterised by its contrast of textures: a shatteringly crisp skin over tender, fatty meat layers. The noodles absorb the rich, umami-forward sauce, creating a deeply savoury flavour profile. Nutritionally, the HPB-analysed per-100g data reflects the meat-dominant component: energy 171 kcal, protein 19.1 g, fat 10.5 g, and carbohydrate 0.0 g — the latter indicating the analysis captured the pork and sauce portion rather than the complete dish with noodles. Sodium is extremely high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is 89 mg/100g and saturated fat is estimated at approximately 3.8 g/100g from the pork belly component. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork content. Clinically, the high sodium and saturated fat content warrant caution for hypertensive, cardiovascular, and rheumatoid arthritis patients. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the noodle component adds a meaningful refined-carbohydrate load not captured in the HPB per-100g figure.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat roasted pork noodle?
Is roasted pork noodle suitable for hypertension?
Roasted pork noodle Singapore hawker food

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010705), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The exact dish entry is present in HPB. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork belly data and documented accordingly. Confidence is high for the HPB-reported values.
notes: ZERO MACRO JUSTIFICATION (g4): carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB. This is confirmed zero per the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific entry (G00010705), and reflects the meat-dominant (roasted pork and sauce) component of the dish as analysed; the noodle component, if included in the full dish as consumed, would contribute significant refined carbohydrate. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB for this entry. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB for this entry, consistent with the meat-dominant analysed portion. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (g3): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, full vitamin panel, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean roasted pork noodle dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry G00010705, and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match to this dish as of 2026. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry #10006 (pork belly, raw, roasted) proportionally adjusted to the HPB-reported total fat of 10.5 g/100g. GI NOTE: The HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g for this record (meat-dominant portion); GI of 52 is assigned as an analogous estimate for the egg noodle component based on the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); glycemic load per serving is 0.0 consistent with the 0 g/100g carbohydrate in this record.
